Yes, the ancient Macedonians used the Greek language, although there were some differences in the local dialect. The Macedonians were part of the wider Greek culture, and the language they used was mainly Greek. In particular, the following points prove the use of the Greek language by the Macedonians:

1. Inscriptions and written testimonies:
Most of the inscriptions that have been found in Macedonia are written in the Greek language. These include public texts, tombstones and administrative documents.
Throughout their history, the Macedonians adopted the Attic dialect of Greek, which was the language of administration and diplomacy during the reigns of Philip II and Alexander the Great.
2. Macedonian dialect:
The ancient Macedonians had a local dialect, which is thought to have been a variant of northwestern Greek. This dialect had some differences from the rest of the Greek dialects, but is considered part of the wider Greek language family.
The language of Macedonia was primarily Greek, although there may have been influences from neighboring peoples due to Macedonia's geographical location.
3. Speech of the Macedonian kings:
Macedonian kings, such as Philip II and Alexander the Great, spoke and wrote in the Greek language. Alexander the Great, in particular, promoted the Greek language and culture throughout the then known world through his conquests.
At the Macedonian royal court, the Attic dialect of Greek was the official language.
4. Participation in Greek cultural and religious events:
Macedonians participated in Greek institutions, such as the Panhellenic Shrines and the Olympic Games, where the Greek language was the medium of communication.
Moreover, the religion and mythology of the Macedonians was in common with that of the other Greeks.
Conclusion:
The ancient Macedonians were part of the Greek world and used the Greek language, both in administration and in everyday life, despite the local linguistic differences that may have existed in the region.
